q: Two stars of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math> at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> rotate about their common centre of mass in free space. The period of revolution is
question: Two stars of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math> at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> rotate about their common centre of mass in free space. The period of revolution is
solution:
F=G2mmd2=2mω2d/3

Gmd2=ω2d3

⇒ω2=3Gmd3

⇒ω=3Gmd3

⇒T=2πω=2πd33Gm

subject: Physics
q: The radii of two planets A and B are R and 4R and their densities are ρ and ρ3 respectively. The ratio of acceleration due to gravity at their surfaces gA:gB will be
question: The radii of two planets A and B are R and 4R and their densities are ρ and ρ3 respectively. The ratio of acceleration due to gravity at their surfaces gA:gB will be
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity is written as g=GMr2=G×43×π×r3×ρr2=4πG3ρr 

Given:

 rA=RrB=4RρA=ρρB=ρ3

Hence, the ratio is 

gAgB=R×ρ4R×ρ3=34.

q: Consider a binary star system of star <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and star <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> with masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></math> revolving in a circular orbit of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></math> are the time period of star <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and star <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively, then:
question: Consider a binary star system of star <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and star <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> with masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></math> revolving in a circular orbit of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></math> are the time period of star <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and star <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively, then:
solution:
1) From the centre of mass, we get

M1R1=M2R2------1

2) Force acting on both starts i.e. gravitational force and centripetal force are balanced.

GM1M2R2=M1V12R1=M1V12R1------2

As we know, V1=2πR1T1&V2=2πR2T2

Substituting equation 3 in equation 2, we get 

M1R1T12=M1R2T22------4

From equation 1, we conclude in eqution 4

T12=T22  or  T1=T2

subject: Physics
q: Two satellites, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>, have masses <math><mi>m</mi></math> and <math><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math> respectively. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is in a circular orbit of radius <math><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math> around the earth. The ratio of their kinetic energies, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><msub><mi>K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is:
question: Two satellites, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>, have masses <math><mi>m</mi></math> and <math><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math> respectively. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is in a circular orbit of radius <math><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math> around the earth. The ratio of their kinetic energies, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><msub><mi>K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is:
solution:
Let v be the orbital velocity of the satellite which is given as v=GMr

Here M be the mass of the earth, m be the mass of satellite and r is the orbit radius.

Kinetic energy, K=12mv2=12mGMr2=GMm2r∝mr

Ratio, KAKB=mAmB×rBrA=m2m×2RR=1

q: Two planets <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of equal mass are having their period of revolutions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>T</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math>. These planets are revolving in the circular orbits of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> respectively. Which out of the following would be the correct relationship of their orbits?
question: Two planets <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of equal mass are having their period of revolutions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>T</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math>. These planets are revolving in the circular orbits of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> respectively. Which out of the following would be the correct relationship of their orbits?
solution:
From Kepler's law of periods,

T2∝R3

Therefore,

⇒TATB2=rArB3

⇒22=rA3rB3, ⇒rA3=4rB3

subject: Physics
q: Take the mean distance of the moon and the sun from the earth to be $0.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~km}$ and $150 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~km}$ respectively. Their masses are $8 \times 10^{22} \mathrm{~kg}$ and $2 \times$ $10^{30} \mathrm{~kg}$ respectively. The radius of the earth is $6400 \mathrm{~km}$. Let $\Delta F_1$ be the difference in the forces exerted by the moon at the nearest and farthest points on the earth and $\Delta \mathrm{F}_2$ be the difference in the force exerted by the sun at the nearest and farthest points on the earth. Then, the number closest to $\frac{\Delta F_1}{\Delta F_2}$ is:
question: Take the mean distance of the moon and the sun from the earth to be $0.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~km}$ and $150 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~km}$ respectively. Their masses are $8 \times 10^{22} \mathrm{~kg}$ and $2 \times$ $10^{30} \mathrm{~kg}$ respectively. The radius of the earth is $6400 \mathrm{~km}$. Let $\Delta F_1$ be the difference in the forces exerted by the moon at the nearest and farthest points on the earth and $\Delta \mathrm{F}_2$ be the difference in the force exerted by the sun at the nearest and farthest points on the earth. Then, the number closest to $\frac{\Delta F_1}{\Delta F_2}$ is:
solution:
As we know, Gravitational force of attraction,
$\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{\mathrm{R}^2}$
$\mathrm{~F}_1=\frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{r}_1^2}$ and $\mathrm{F}_2=\frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{r}_2^2}$
$\Delta \mathrm{F}_1=\frac{2 \mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{r}_1^3} \Delta \mathrm{r}_1$ and $\Delta \mathrm{F}_2=\frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{r}_2^3} \Delta \mathrm{r}_2$
Using $\Delta \mathrm{r}_1=\Delta \mathrm{r}_2=2 \mathrm{R}_{\text {earth }} ; \mathrm{m}=8 \times 10^{22} \mathrm{~kg}$;
$\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{s}}=2 \times 10^{30} \mathrm{~kg}$
$\mathrm{r}_1=0.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~km}^2$ and $\mathrm{r}_2=150 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~km}$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{F}_1}{\Delta \mathrm{F}_2}=\left(\frac{8 \times 10^{22}}{2 \times 10^{30}}\right)\left(\frac{150 \times 10^6}{0.4 \times 10^6}\right)^3 \times 1 \cong 2$

subject: Physics
q: A satellite is moving in a low nearly circular orbit around the earth. Its radius is roughly equal to that of the earth's radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> By firing rockets attached to it, its speed is instantaneously increased in the direction of its motion so that it become <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msqrt></math> times larger. Due to this the farthest distance from the centre of the earth that the satellite reaches is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math>. Value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is :
question: A satellite is moving in a low nearly circular orbit around the earth. Its radius is roughly equal to that of the earth's radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> By firing rockets attached to it, its speed is instantaneously increased in the direction of its motion so that it become <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msqrt></math> times larger. Due to this the farthest distance from the centre of the earth that the satellite reaches is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math>. Value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is :
solution:
V=GMRe

From angular momentum conversation

-GMmRe+12m32V2=-GMmRmax+12mVmin2

From angular momentum conversation

32VRe=VminRmax

Eliminating  Vmin  from equation (i) and (ii) we get

Rmax=3Re

subject: Physics
q: The change in the value of $g$ at a height ' $h$ ' above the surface of the earth is the same as at a depth 'd' below the surface of earth. When both ' $d$ ' and ' $h$ ' are much smaller than the radius of earth, then which one of the following is correct?
question: The change in the value of $g$ at a height ' $h$ ' above the surface of the earth is the same as at a depth 'd' below the surface of earth. When both ' $d$ ' and ' $h$ ' are much smaller than the radius of earth, then which one of the following is correct?
solution:
$\frac{G M}{(R+h)^2}=\frac{G M}{R^3}(R-d)$
$\Rightarrow d=2 h$

question:

solution:
Energy given =Uf-Ui

x=3

subject: Physics
q: In hydrogen like system the ratio of coul0mbian force and gravitational force between an electron and a proton is in the order of :
question: In hydrogen like system the ratio of coul0mbian force and gravitational force between an electron and a proton is in the order of :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  } \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{\mathrm{kQ}_1 \mathrm{Q}_2}{\mathrm{r}^2}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{\mathrm{r}^2} \\ & \mathrm{~F}_{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{\mathrm{Gm}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2}{\mathrm{r}^2}=\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27}}{\mathrm{r}^2} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{g}}} \cong 0.23 \times 10^{40} \cong 2.3 \times 10^{39} \\ & \text { Option (1) }\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The time period of a satellite, revolving above earth's surface at a height equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> will be (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of earth)
question: The time period of a satellite, revolving above earth's surface at a height equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> will be (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of earth)
solution:
The velocity of the satellite revolving round the Earth at a height r from the centre of the Earth is given by

v=GMr   ...1

The formula to calculate the time period in the circular orbit is given by

T=2πrv   ...2

In this case: r=R+R=2R

Substitute the expression for the velocity from equation (1) into equation (2) and simplify to obtain the required time period.

T=2πrGMr=2π×2RgR2=4πR2πR=42R=32R

subject: Physics
q: Two particles of equal mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> move in a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> under the action of their mutual gravitational attraction. The speed of each particle will be :
question: Two particles of equal mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> move in a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> under the action of their mutual gravitational attraction. The speed of each particle will be :
solution:
Let speed of both the particles be v.

Then, gravitational force between the particles will provide the required centripetal force.

Therefore,

Gmm2r2=mv2r

⇒v=Gm4r

subject: Physics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the radius of the earth and the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the length of the second's pendulum at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math> from the surface of earth will be:
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the radius of the earth and the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the length of the second's pendulum at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math> from the surface of earth will be:
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at the given height, g'=GM3R2=19GMR2=19g, where g is acceleration due to gravity at the surface of the Earth.

Now, time period is given by, T=2πlg'

Also the time period of second pendulum is 2 sec.

Therefore, T=2 sec

Then, 2=2πlg×9

⇒l=19 m

subject: Physics
q: The kinetic energy needed to project a body of mass $m$ from the earth surface (radius $R$ ) to infinity is
question: The kinetic energy needed to project a body of mass $m$ from the earth surface (radius $R$ ) to infinity is
solution: $\mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{esc}}^2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}(\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gR}})^2=\mathrm{mgR}$

subject: Physics
q: A light planet is revolving around a massive star in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> with a period of revolution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the force of attraction between planet and star is proportional to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>R</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> then choose the correct option :
question: A light planet is revolving around a massive star in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> with a period of revolution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the force of attraction between planet and star is proportional to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>R</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> then choose the correct option :
solution:
Let the gravitational force of attraction be F=GMmR3/2.

Now, this gravitational force will provide the required centripetal force. Therefore, 

GMmR3/2=mω2R

⇒ω2∝1R5/2  ∵T=2πω

⇒T2∝R5/2

subject: Physics
q: Planet <math><mi>A</mi></math> has mass <math><mi>M</mi></math> and radius <math><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Planet <math><mi>B</mi></math> has half the mass and half the radius of Planet <math><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the escape velocities from the Planets <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math> are <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> respectively, then <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math><mi>n</mi></math> is:
question: Planet <math><mi>A</mi></math> has mass <math><mi>M</mi></math> and radius <math><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Planet <math><mi>B</mi></math> has half the mass and half the radius of Planet <math><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the escape velocities from the Planets <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math> are <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> respectively, then <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math><mi>n</mi></math> is:
solution:
ve=2GMR
∴vAvB=2GMR2GM/2R/2=1=n4
⇒n=4

A satellite of mass m is projected from the surface of the planet of mass M directly towards the centre of the second planet. The minimum speed v required for the satellite to reach the surface of the second planet is a7GMR, then the value of  a is
[Given : The two planets are fixed in their position]
question:
Suppose two planets (spherical in shape) of radii R and 2R, but mass M and 9M respectively have a centre to centre separation 8R as shown in the figure. A satellite of mass m is projected from the surface of the planet of mass M directly towards the centre of the second planet. The minimum speed v required for the satellite to reach the surface of the second planet is a7GMR, then the value of  a is
[Given : The two planets are fixed in their position]
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity will be zero at P therefore,
GMx2=G9M(8R-x)2
8R-x=3x
x=2R

Apply conservation of energy and consider velocity at P is zero.

12mv2-GMmR-G9Mm7R=0-GMm2R-G9Mm6R
∴ V=47GMR

subject: Physics
q: When a body slides down from rest along a smooth inclined plane making an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal, it takes time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When the same body slides down from the rest along a rough inclined plane making the same angle and through the same distance, it takes time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mi>T</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is a constant greater than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The co-efficient of friction between the body and the rough plane is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt></mfrac><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: When a body slides down from rest along a smooth inclined plane making an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal, it takes time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When the same body slides down from the rest along a rough inclined plane making the same angle and through the same distance, it takes time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mi>T</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is a constant greater than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The co-efficient of friction between the body and the rough plane is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt></mfrac><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
On smooth incline,

a=gsin30°

by S=ut+12at2

S=12g2T2=g4T2 ...(i)

On rough incline

a=gsin30°-μgcos30°

by S=ut+12at2

S=14 g(1-3μ)(αT)2  ...(ii)

By (i) and (ii)

14gT2=14g(1-3μ)α2T2

⇒1-3μ=1α2 ⇒μ=α2-1α213

⇒x=3.00

subject: Physics
q: Spherical insulating ball and a spherical metallic ball of same size and mass are dropped from the same height. Choose the correct statement out of the following {Assume negligible air friction}
question: Spherical insulating ball and a spherical metallic ball of same size and mass are dropped from the same height. Choose the correct statement out of the following {Assume negligible air friction}
options: Time taken by them to reach the earth's surface will be independent of the properties of their materials
Insulating ball will reach the earth's surface earlier than the metal ball
Both will reach the earth's surface simultaneously
Metal ball will reach the earth's surface earlier than the insulating ball
solution: When a spherical insulating ball and a spherical metallic ball of same size and mass are dropped from the same height, then magnetic field will act on metallic ball. If the earth's magnetic field is taken into account, then an induced eddy current will form inside the spherical metallic ball, thus retarding its motion. Hence, it will take more time than insulating ball.

q: A body A of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> is moving in a circular orbit of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> about a planet. Another body B of mass <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> collides with A with a velocity which is half <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> the instantaneous velocity <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> of A. The collision is completely inelastic. Then, the combined body:
question: A body A of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> is moving in a circular orbit of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> about a planet. Another body B of mass <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> collides with A with a velocity which is half <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> the instantaneous velocity <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> of A. The collision is completely inelastic. Then, the combined body:
options: continues to move in a circular orbit
Escapes from the Planet's Gravitational field
Falls vertically downwards towards the planet
starts moving in an elliptical orbit around the planet
solution:
Conserving momentum
m2v2+mv=m+m2vf
vf=2mV4×3m2=5V6
vf<vorb=v thus the combined mass will go on to an elliptical path

subject: Physics
q: At a certain depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> below surface of earth, value of acceleration due to gravity becomes four times that of its value at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math> above earth surface. Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is Radius of earth (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>). The depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> is equal to
question: At a certain depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> below surface of earth, value of acceleration due to gravity becomes four times that of its value at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math> above earth surface. Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is Radius of earth (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>). The depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
The magnitude of acceleration due to gravity g1 at a depth of d below the surface of Earth is

g1=GMR3R-d=GMR21-dR    ....(1)

The magnitude of acceleration due to gravity g2 at a height d above the surface of Earth is

g2=GMR+d2    ....(2)

Given here, g1=4g2

GMR21-dR=4GMR+3R2

⇒GMR21-dR=4GM4R2

⇒1-dR=14⇒dR=34⇒d=34R

⇒d=4800 km

q: Four identical particles of equal masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> made to move along the circumference of a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> under the action of their own mutual gravitational attraction. The speed of each particle will be:
question: Four identical particles of equal masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> made to move along the circumference of a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> under the action of their own mutual gravitational attraction. The speed of each particle will be:
solution:
F1=Gmm2R2=Gm24R2

 F2=Gmm2R2=Gm22R2

 F3=Gmm2R2=Gm22R2

⇒Fnet=F1+F2cos45°+F3cos45°

=Gm24R2+Gm22R212+Gm22R212

=Gm2R214+12=Gm24R21+22

Fnet=Gm24R21+22=mv2R

⇒v=G1+222

subject: Physics
q:
A planet revolving in elliptical orbit has :

A. a constant velocity of revolution.

B. has the least velocity when it is nearest to the sun.

C. its areal velocity is directly proportional to its velocity.

D. areal velocity is inversely proportional to its velocity.

E. to follow a trajectory such that the areal velocity is constant.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
A planet revolving in elliptical orbit has :

A. a constant velocity of revolution.

B. has the least velocity when it is nearest to the sun.

C. its areal velocity is directly proportional to its velocity.

D. areal velocity is inversely proportional to its velocity.

E. to follow a trajectory such that the areal velocity is constant.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution: As per Kepler’s second law “The areal velocity of a planet revolving around the sun in elliptical orbit remains constant which implies the angular momentum of a planet remains constant”. Hence (option E) is the correct option.

subject: Physics
q: Assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform mass density, the weight of a body at a depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> from the surface of earth, if its weight on the surface of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, will be : (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of earth)
question: Assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform mass density, the weight of a body at a depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> from the surface of earth, if its weight on the surface of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, will be : (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of earth)
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity with depth is 

g'=g1-dR

The given data is 

W=200 Nd=R2

Let the weight be at the given depth, W'. Hence, the value is 

mg'=mg1-R2R=mg2

The Weight W' =W2=2002=100 N

subject: Physics
q: Correct formula for height of a satellite from earths surface is:
question: Correct formula for height of a satellite from earths surface is:
solution: $\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h})^2}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h})}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{GM}}{(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h})}=\mathrm{v}^2$ ....(1) $\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h}) \omega$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h}) \frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{T}}$ ....(2) $\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}^2}=\mathrm{g}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{GM}=\mathrm{gR}^2$ ....(3) Put value from (2) \& (3) in eq. (1) $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{gR}^2}{(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h})}=(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h})^2\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{T}}\right)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{T}^2 \mathrm{R}^2 \mathrm{~g}}{(2 \pi)^2}=(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h})^3 \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\frac{\mathrm{T}^2 \mathrm{R}^2 \mathrm{~g}}{(2 \pi)^2}\right]^{1 / 3}-\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{h} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A metal wire of uniform mass density having length $L$ and mass $M$ is bent to form a semicircular arc and a particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ is placed at the centre of the arc. The gravitational force on the particle by the wire is :
question: A metal wire of uniform mass density having length $L$ and mass $M$ is bent to form a semicircular arc and a particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ is placed at the centre of the arc. The gravitational force on the particle by the wire is :
solution:
We have $R=\frac{L}{\pi}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{g}_0=\frac{2 \mathrm{G} \frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{L}}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{GM} \pi}{\mathrm{L}^2} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{m}}=\mathrm{mg}_0=\frac{2 \mathrm{GM} \pi \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{L}^2} \end{aligned}$
Hence option (4)

If the gravitational potential at a point, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the center is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is:
question: A mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is placed at the center of a uniform spherical shell of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If the gravitational potential at a point, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the center is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is:
solution: Use the formula of the gravitational potential in terms of universal gravitational constant, V=-GMR⇒Vp=-G(100)50-G(50)25=-4G.

q: Take the mean distance of the moon and the sun from the earth to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, respectively. Their masses are <math><mi> </mi><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>22</mn></mrow></msup></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>30</mn></mrow></msup></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi></math>, respectively. The radius of the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. Let <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><msub><mrow><mi>F</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> be the difference in the forces exerted by the moon at the nearest and farthest point on the earth, and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><msub><mrow><mi>F</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> be the difference in the forces exerted by the sun at the nearest and farthest points on the earth. Then, the number closest to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> is,
question: Take the mean distance of the moon and the sun from the earth to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, respectively. Their masses are <math><mi> </mi><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>22</mn></mrow></msup></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>30</mn></mrow></msup></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi></math>, respectively. The radius of the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. Let <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><msub><mrow><mi>F</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> be the difference in the forces exerted by the moon at the nearest and farthest point on the earth, and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><msub><mrow><mi>F</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> be the difference in the forces exerted by the sun at the nearest and farthest points on the earth. Then, the number closest to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> is,
solution:
F1=GMemr13,F2=GMeMsr22 

ΔF1= (-)2GMemr13 Δr1 , ΔF2=(-)2GMeMsr22 Δr2

ΔF1ΔF2= mΔr1r13 r23MsΔr2=mMsr23r13Δr1Δr2

Using Δr1=Δr2=2Rearth

m=8×1022 kg

Ms= 2×1030 kg

r1=0.4×106 km

r2=150×106 km

We get ΔF1ΔF2=2.

subject: Physics
q:
The ratio of escape velocity of a planet to the escape velocity of earth will be:-

Given: Mass of the planet is 16 times mass of earth and radius of the planet is 4 times the radius of earth.
question:
The ratio of escape velocity of a planet to the escape velocity of earth will be:-

Given: Mass of the planet is 16 times mass of earth and radius of the planet is 4 times the radius of earth.
solution:
The formula to calculate the escape velocity is given by

ve=2GMR   ...1

For the Earth, it is given by

veE=2GMERE...2

 and, for the planet, it is given by

veP=2GMPRP...3

Divide equation (2) by equation (3) and simplify to obtain the required ratio.

veEveP=MEMP×RPRE   ...4

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (4) to obtain the required ratio.

veEveP=ME16ME×4RERE=12⇒veP:veE= 2:1

subject: Physics
q: The distance between Sun and Earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. The duration of year if the distance between Sun and Earth becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math> will be :
question: The distance between Sun and Earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. The duration of year if the distance between Sun and Earth becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math> will be :
solution:
According to Kepler's Third Law – The Law of Periods,

         T2∝R3

where T is time taken by the planet to go once around the sun and R is the semi-major axis (distance) of the elliptical orbit.

∴ T2=k R3   …(i)

Where k is a constant of proportionality.

When R becomes 3 times, let the time period be T'.

∴                    T'2=k(3R)3             …(ii)

∴                    T2T'2=127

So, time period will be 33 yr.
